agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics platform feature that automatically plans and dynamically re-plans driver delivery routes within geographic zones, integrating vehicle capability constraints, real-time recalculation triggers, and proactive customer notifications. This is a core operational feature sitting at the intersection of route planning, fleet management, delivery lifecycle, and customer communication.","bounded_context":"This feature spans at least four bounded contexts: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, optimization criteria, recalculation triggers, and stop sequencing; (2) Fleet & Driver Management — owns driver shifts, vehicle class licensing, zone assignments; (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle (pending, in-progress, failed, completed), zone allocation; (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers and customer-facing delivery status. The primary context for this feature is Route Planning, which consumes data from the others via well-defined interfaces.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Optimized Route","definition":"An ordered sequence of delivery stops assigned to a driver, calculated to minimize travel time/distance within the driver's zone for their current shift."},{"term":"Zone","definition":"A geographic area used to partition deliveries and assign them to drivers. A delivery belongs to exactly one zone."},{"term":"Shift","definition":"A bounded period during which a driver is active and eligible to receive route assignments. A shift has a start and end."},{"term":"Recalculation Trigger","definition":"An event that invalidates the current optimized route and forces the system to recompute it. Three defined triggers: new delivery added, delivery marked failed, driver deviation."},{"term":"Driver Deviation","definition":"When a driver does not follow the next prescribed stop in the optimized route sequence (e.g., skips a stop or visits stops out of order)."},{"term":"Next-3-Stops Window","definition":"The upcoming three delivery stops in the driver's current optimized route, used to trigger proactive customer notifications."},{"term":"Vehicle Class","definition":"A classification of vehicle capability (e.g., motorcycle, van, truck, freight). Determines which deliveries a driver is eligible to fulfill."},{"term":"Licensed Vehicle Class","definition":"The vehicle class(es) a driver is authorized and equipped to operate during their shift."},{"term":"Stop Time","definition":"The estimated duration a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes but is overridable per zone by the operations team."},{"term":"Pending Delivery","definition":"A delivery that has been created and assigned to a zone but not yet assigned to a driver's route or completed."},{"term":"Failed Delivery","definition":"A delivery attempt that was unsuccessful (e.g., customer not available), triggering route recalculation to remove or reschedule the stop."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"When a driver starts their shift, the system must automatically generate an optimized route from all pending deliveries in the driver's assigned zone.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational workflow trigger, not a data integrity constraint. The optimization algorithm and trigger timing are policy decisions.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN pending deliveries exist in zone X, WHEN a driver assigned to zone X starts their shift, THEN an optimized route containing all eligible pending deliveries is created and assigned to the driver within the system's SLA."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A driver's route must be recalculated when a new delivery is added to their zone while they are on an active shift.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Recalculation policy can be tuned (e.g., batching, cooldown periods) without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a driver has an active route in zone X, WHEN a new pending delivery is added to zone X, THEN the route is recalculated incorporating the new delivery while preserving already-completed stops."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"A driver's route must be recalculated when a delivery on their route is marked as failed.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Recalculation on failure is a policy; the system could instead just skip the stop.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a driver has an active route containing delivery D, WHEN delivery D is marked as failed, THEN the route is recalculated excluding delivery D from remaining stops."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A driver's route must be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Deviation handling is a business policy; alternative approaches (warning, ignoring) are valid choices.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a driver has an active route with next stop S, WHEN the driver skips S or visits a different stop, THEN the route is recalculated from the driver's current position with remaining undelivered stops."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Customers must be notified when their delivery enters the next-3-stops window of a driver's optimized route.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Notification window size (3 stops) is a configurable business preference, not a system integrity rule.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a driver is progressing through their route, WHEN a customer's delivery becomes one of the next 3 stops in the sequence, THEN the customer receives a notification."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"A driver must never be assigned a delivery that requires a vehicle class exceeding their licensed vehicle class.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Assigning a motorcycle driver a freight shipment is physically impossible and likely illegal. Violating this creates safety hazards, regulatory violations, and guaranteed delivery failures. This is a hard physical and legal constraint.","testable_assertion":"FOR every delivery D on a driver's route, D.requiredVehicleClass must be compatible with driver.licensedVehicleClass. No route — whether initial or recalculated — may ever contain an incompatible assignment."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"The default estimated stop time is 4 minutes per delivery stop, overridable by the operations team per zone.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Stop time is a tunable operational parameter, not a system constraint.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN no override exists for zone X, WHEN calculating route ETAs, THEN 4 minutes per stop is used. GIVEN an override of N minutes exists for zone X, WHEN calculating route ETAs, THEN N minutes per stop is used."},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"Route recalculation must not reorder or revisit stops that the driver has already completed.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Re-assigning completed deliveries corrupts delivery state and causes duplicate delivery attempts, wasting resources and confusing customers.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a driver has completed stops [A, B, C], WHEN route recalculation occurs, THEN the new route contains only uncompleted stops and completed stops are excluded."},{"id":"BR-9","rule":"A delivery can only appear on one driver's active route at a time.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Two drivers attempting the same delivery wastes resources, creates customer confusion, and corrupts delivery state tracking.","testable_assertion":"FOR any point in time, a delivery D that is assigned to driver X's active route must not appear on any other driver's active route."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A driver assigned to zone X starts their shift and there are 12 pending deliveries in zone X","when":"The shift is activated in the system","then":"An optimized route is generated containing all 12 deliveries, ordered to minimize travel, and assigned to the driver. Each delivery is marked as routed."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A driver has an active route with 8 remaining stops in zone X","when":"A 9th pending delivery is added to zone X","then":"The route is recalculated to include the new delivery among the remaining stops. Completed stops are untouched. The driver sees the updated route."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A driver has an active route and delivery D is the 5th of 8 remaining stops","when":"Delivery D is marked as failed","then":"The route is recalculated excluding D. Remaining stops are re-optimized. Customer for D is not notified of imminent delivery."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A driver's optimized route says next stop is Stop A","when":"The driver proceeds to Stop C instead (deviation)","then":"The system detects the deviation, recalculates the route from the driver's current position, and reorders remaining unvisited stops."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A driver is completing stops and delivery D is currently the 4th upcoming stop","when":"The driver completes a stop, making D now the 3rd upcoming stop","then":"Customer for delivery D receives a notification that their delivery is approaching."},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A motorcycle driver is assigned to zone X and a freight shipment is added to zone X","when":"Route calculation or recalculation runs","then":"The freight shipment is excluded from the motorcycle driver's route. The shipment remains pending for an eligible driver."},{"id":"AC-7","given":"Zone X has a stop-time override of 6 minutes set by operations","when":"A route is calculated for a driver in zone X","then":"All ETA calculations use 6 minutes per stop instead of the default 4 minutes."},{"id":"AC-8","given":"A driver has completed 5 of 10 stops and a recalculation is triggered","when":"The recalculation completes","then":"The new route contains exactly the 5 uncompleted stops. The 5 completed stops do not appear."},{"id":"AC-9","given":"A route recalculation changes the upcoming stop order such that customer C was in the next-3 window but is now stop #5","when":"The recalculated route is applied","then":"Customer C does not receive a duplicate notification. The system reflects the updated window accurately."},{"id":"AC-10","given":"A driver starts their shift in zone X but there are zero pending deliveries","when":"The shift is activated","then":"The driver is placed in an active-waiting state with no route. When a delivery is later added to zone X, a route is generated."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"No pending deliveries when driver starts shift","expected_behavior":"Driver enters an idle/waiting state. Route is generated as soon as the first delivery appears in the zone."},{"scenario":"Multiple recalculation triggers fire simultaneously (new delivery added + delivery failed at same time)","expected_behavior":"Recalculations must be serialized or debounced to avoid race conditions. The resulting route must reflect all triggers, not just the first."},{"scenario":"Driver deviates to a stop that IS on their route but out of order","expected_behavior":"System should recognize the stop as completed (not a true deviation to an unknown location), mark it done, and recalculate remaining stops from current position."},{"scenario":"Customer notification sent, then recalculation moves their delivery out of next-3 window","expected_behavior":"No retraction notification is explicitly specified. System must avoid re-notifying the same customer if they re-enter the window later. Idempotent notifications."},{"scenario":"All remaining deliveries on a route are marked as failed","expected_behavior":"Route becomes empty. Driver returns to idle/waiting state, eligible for new deliveries."},{"scenario":"A delivery is added to a zone with multiple active drivers","expected_behavior":"System must decide which driver gets the delivery. Assignment logic must consider proximity, current load, vehicle class, and route efficiency. Delivery must appear on exactly one route."},{"scenario":"Driver's shift ends while deliveries remain on route","expected_behavior":"Undelivered stops must be released back to pending status, available for reassignment. Customers in next-3 window who were notified may need a status update."},{"scenario":"Operations team changes zone stop-time override mid-shift","expected_behavior":"Should trigger route ETA recalculation for all active routes in the zone to maintain accurate arrival estimates."},{"scenario":"Recalculation cascade: recalculating route A causes a delivery to shift, triggering recalculation of route B","expected_behavior":"System must have cycle detection or a maximum recalculation depth to prevent infinite loops."},{"scenario":"Zone boundary delivery — delivery location is on the border of two zones","expected_behavior":"Delivery must be assigned to exactly one zone. Ambiguous zone membership must be resolved deterministically."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, increases deliveries per shift (higher throughput per driver), and reduces missed delivery costs. Vehicle class enforcement prevents costly failed delivery attempts and potential liability from safety violations.","operational":"Dynamic recalculation increases dispatcher efficiency by automating what was likely a manual process. However, frequent recalculations could cause driver confusion and route instability ('route thrashing'). The 4-minute default stop time creates a baseline for capacity planning.","user":"Customers benefit from proactive next-3-stops notifications, improving delivery experience and reducing 'where is my package' inquiries. Drivers benefit from automated route planning but may be frustrated by frequent route changes mid-shift.","risk":"Vehicle class mismatch is a safety and regulatory risk (HIGH). Route thrashing from excessive recalculations is an operational risk (MEDIUM). Notification accuracy impacts customer trust (MEDIUM). Zone assignment logic for multi-driver zones is an algorithmic fairness risk (LOW-MEDIUM).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Drivers","impact":"Core workflow change — shift from manual/dispatcher-planned routes to system-generated dynamic routes. Requires trust in optimization quality. Frequent recalculations could cause frustration."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Improved visibility via next-3-stops notifications. Higher delivery reliability from optimized routes. Risk of notification noise if recalculations are frequent."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"New configuration responsibility (zone stop-time overrides). Reduced manual dispatch workload. Need monitoring tools for route quality and recalculation frequency."},{"group":"Dispatchers","impact":"Role significantly changes — system handles primary route assignment. Dispatchers shift to exception handling and oversight."},{"group":"Fleet Managers","impact":"Vehicle class data must be accurate and up-to-date, as it now enforces hard constraints on delivery assignment."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The spec is well-structured but contains several ambiguities that materially affect implementation: the definition of 'deviation' is vague, optimization criteria are unspecified, multi-driver zone assignment logic is undefined, and the interaction between recalculation triggers and customer notifications needs clarification. No contradictions exist, but the ambiguities span critical operational paths.","high_confidence_areas":["Vehicle class constraint is a hard invariant — unambiguous and safety-critical","Recalculation trigger list is explicitly enumerated (3 triggers)","Stop time default and override mechanism is clearly specified","Customer notification trigger (next-3-stops) is well-defined","Completed stops must not be revisited — clear invariant"],"low_confidence_areas":["Optimization criteria (minimize distance? time? priority-weighted?) never stated","Multi-driver zone assignment — how to choose which driver gets a new delivery","Shift lifecycle — what happens to undelivered stops when shift ends","Notification idempotency — behavior when recalculation changes the next-3 window","Recalculation frequency limits — no debounce or throttle policy specified"],"assumptions":["A delivery belongs to exactly one zone and does not move between zones","A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift","Vehicle class compatibility is a strict hierarchy or categorization maintained in the Fleet context","Route optimization considers travel distance/time as the primary criterion","Notifications are one-way (push to customer) with no acknowledgment required"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Driver manually deviates from the suggested route' is undefined. Deviation could mean: (a) driver visits a stop out of sequence, (b) driver takes a different road between stops, (c) driver goes to a location not on their route. Each interpretation requires different detection logic (GPS geofencing vs. stop-sequence tracking) and produces different recalculation behavior.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Operations Team","Engineering"],"options":["Define deviation as visiting stops out of prescribed sequence (stop-order deviation)","Define deviation as GPS position diverging from prescribed path by threshold distance (geographic deviation)","Define deviation as both — out-of-sequence stop completion OR geographic divergence"],"recommendation":"Define deviation as stop-order deviation (visiting stops out of sequence or skipping a stop), as this is detectable without continuous GPS tracking and aligns with the route-as-stop-sequence model. Geographic path deviation between stops should not trigger recalculation."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Optimization criteria for route calculation are never specified. 'Optimized route' could mean shortest distance, shortest time, delivery-priority-weighted, or a hybrid. The choice fundamentally changes route behavior and customer experience.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Customers","Operations Team"],"options":["Optimize for shortest total travel time","Optimize for shortest total distance","Optimize for delivery priority (SLA-sensitive deliveries first) with time as tiebreaker","Multi-objective optimization with configurable weights per zone"],"recommendation":"Optimize for shortest total travel time with delivery-priority constraints (SLA deadlines must be met first, then minimize time). This balances efficiency with customer commitments."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"When multiple drivers are active in the same zone and a new delivery is added, the spec does not define which driver receives the delivery. This is a critical assignment decision that affects route quality, driver workload fairness, and delivery timeliness.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Customers","Operations Team","Dispatchers"],"options":["Assign to the driver with the fewest remaining stops (load balancing)","Assign to the driver geographically closest to the new delivery (proximity)","Assign to the driver whose route would increase least in total time (optimization-based)","Let dispatchers manually assign, with system suggestion"],"recommendation":"Use optimization-based assignment (least total time increase) with a load-balancing cap to prevent one driver from receiving disproportionate stops. This maximizes system efficiency while maintaining fairness."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The interaction between recalculation and the next-3-stops notification is ambiguous. If a customer was notified (entered the window), then a recalculation pushes them out of the window, should a retraction/update be sent? If they re-enter the window later, should they be notified again?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Operations Team"],"options":["Notifications are fire-and-forget — no retraction, no duplicate","Send update notification if customer leaves window after being notified","Notifications are idempotent — customer is notified at most once per delivery"],"recommendation":"Notifications should be idempotent (at-most-once per delivery). Once a customer is notified, they are not notified again for the same delivery regardless of window changes. No retraction notifications — they create more confusion than clarity."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics platform feature that automatically plans and dynamically re-plans driver delivery routes within geographic zones, integrating vehicle capability constraints, real-time recalculation triggers, and proactive customer notifications. This is a core operational feature sitting at the intersection of route planning, fleet management, delivery lifecycle, and customer communication.","bounded_context":"This feature spans at least four bounded contexts

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this logistics domain scenario carefully.

The feature "Smart Route Optimization" spans multiple bounded contexts. Let me identify them and the business rules.

Bounded contexts:
1. Route Planning / Optimization - route calculation, recalculation triggers
2. Fleet/Driver Management - vehicle class licensing, shift management
3. Delivery Management - delivery lifecycle, zone assignment
4. Customer Notification - next-3-stops window

Let me think about the business rules, invariants, and edge cases.

This is a well-specified feature request. There are some ambiguities I should flag, but no contradictions. Let me identify them:


Several key details need clarification: what exactly counts as a route deviation that warrants recalculation, how zones are defined and whether they can overlap, and who manages the vehicle class licensing data. I'm also uncertain about whether the next-3-stops notification is tied to the optimized sequence and what happens if recalculation shifts those stops, plus whether the operations team's override of stop times applies zone-wide or at a more granular level. The optimization criteria itself seems incomplete—I need to understand what metrics are being prioritized beyond just distance.

These are all ambiguities that could be interpreted in multiple ways, so I'm flagging them as requiring clarification before moving forward with the full analysis.

Need monitoring tools for route quality and recalculation frequency."},{"group":"Dispatchers","impact":"Role significantly changes — system handles primary route assignment. Dispatchers shift to exception handling and oversight."},{"group":"Fleet Managers","impact":"Vehicle class data must be accurate and up-to-date, as it now enforces hard constraints on delivery assignment."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The spec is well-structured but contains several ambiguities that materially affect implementation: the definition of 'deviation' is vague, optimization criteria are unspecified, multi-driver zone assignment logic is undefined, and the interaction between recalculation triggers and customer notifications needs clarification. No contradictions exist, but the ambiguities span critical operational paths.","high_confidence_areas":["Vehicle class constraint is a hard invariant — unambiguous and safety-critical","Recalculation trigger list is explicitly enumerated (3 triggers)","Stop time default and override mechanism is clearly specified","Customer notification trigger (next-3-stops) is well-defined","Completed stops must not be revisited — clear invariant"],"low_confidence_areas":["Optimization criteria (minimize distance? time? priority-weighted?) never stated","Multi-driver zone assignment — how to choose which driver gets a new delivery","Shift lifecycle — what happens to undelivered stops when shift ends","Notification idempotency — behavior when recalculation changes the next-3 window","Recalculation frequency limits — no debounce or throttle policy specified"],"assumptions":["A delivery belongs to exactly one zone and does not move between zones","A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift","Vehicle class compatibility is a strict hierarchy or categorization maintained in the Fleet context","Route optimization considers travel distance/time as the primary criterion","Notifications are one-way (push to customer) with no acknowledgment required"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Driver manually deviates from the suggested route' is undefined. Deviation could mean: (a) driver visits a stop out of sequence, (b) driver takes a different road between stops, (c) driver goes to a location not on their route. Each interpretation requires different detection logic (GPS geofencing vs. stop-sequence tracking) and produces different recalculation behavior.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Operations Team","Engineering"],"options":["Define deviation as visiting stops out of prescribed sequence (stop-order deviation)","Define deviation as GPS position diverging from prescribed path by threshold distance (geographic deviation)","Define deviation as both — out-of-sequence stop completion OR geographic divergence"],"recommendation":"Define deviation as stop-order deviation (visiting stops out of sequence or skipping a stop), as this is detectable without continuous GPS tracking and aligns with the route-as-stop-sequence model. Geographic path deviation between stops should not trigger recalculation."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Optimization criteria for route calculation are never specified. 'Optimized route' could mean shortest distance, shortest time, delivery-priority-weighted, or a hybrid. The choice fundamentally changes route behavior and customer experience.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Customers","Operations Team"],"options":["Optimize for shortest total travel time","Optimize for shortest total distance","Optimize for delivery priority (SLA-sensitive deliveries first) with time as tiebreaker","Multi-objective optimization with configurable weights per zone"],"recommendation":"Optimize for shortest total travel time with delivery-priority constraints (SLA deadlines must be met first, then minimize time). This balances efficiency with customer commitments."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"When multiple drivers are active in the same zone and a new delivery is added, the spec does not define which driver receives the delivery. This is a critical assignment decision that affects route quality, driver workload fairness, and delivery timeliness.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Customers","Operations Team","Dispatchers"],"options":["Assign to the driver with the fewest remaining stops (load balancing)","Assign to the driver geographically closest to the new delivery (proximity)","Assign to the driver whose route would increase least in total time (optimization-based)","Let dispatchers manually assign, with system suggestion"],"recommendation":"Use optimization-based assignment (least total time increase) with a load-balancing cap to prevent one driver from receiving disproportionate stops. This maximizes system efficiency while maintaining fairness."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The interaction between recalculation and the next-3-stops notification is ambiguous. If a customer was notified (entered the window), then a recalculation pushes them out of the window, should a retraction/update be sent? If they re-enter the window later, should they be notified again?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Operations Team"],"options":["Notifications are fire-and-forget — no retraction, no duplicate","Send update notification if customer leaves window after being notified","Notifications are idempotent — customer is notified at most once per delivery"],"recommendation":"Notifications should be idempotent (at-most-once per delivery). Once a customer is notified, they are not notified again for the same delivery regardless of window changes. No retraction notifications — they create more confusion than clarity."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

trace.result: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics platform feature that automatically plans and dynamically re-plans driver delivery routes within geographic zones, integrating vehicle capability constraints, real-time recalculation triggers, and proactive customer notifications. This is a core operational feature sitting at the intersection of route planning, fleet management, delivery lifecycle, and customer communication.","bounded_context":"This feature spans at least four bounded contexts: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, optimization criteria, recalculation triggers, and stop sequencing; (2) Fleet & Driver Management — owns driver shifts, vehicle class licensing, zone assignments; (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle (pending, in-progress, failed, completed), zone allocation; (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers and customer-facing delivery status. The primary context for this feature is Route Planning, which consumes data from the others via well-defined interfaces.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Optimized Route","definition":"An ordered sequence of delivery stops assigned to a driver, calculated to minimize travel time/distance within the driver's zone for their current shift."},{"term":"Zone","definition":"A geographic area used to partition deliveries and assign them to drivers. A delivery belongs to exactly one zone."},{"term":"Shift","definition":"A bounded period during which a driver is active and eligible to receive route assignments. A shift has a start and end."},{"term":"Recalculation Trigger","definition":"An event that invalidates the current optimized route and forces the system to recompute it. Three defined triggers: new delivery added, delivery marked failed, driver deviation."},{"term":"Driver Deviation","definition":"When a driver does not follow the next prescribed stop in the optimized route sequence (e.g., skips a stop or visits stops out of order)."},{"term":"Next-3-Stops Window","definition":"The upcoming three delivery stops in the driver's current optimized route, used to trigger proactive customer notifications."},{"term":"Vehicle Class","definition":"A classification of vehicle capability (e.g., motorcycle, van, truck, freight). Determines which deliveries a driver is eligible to fulfill."},{"term":"Licensed Vehicle Class","definition":"The vehicle class(es) a driver is authorized and equipped to operate during their shift."},{"term":"Stop Time","definition":"The estimated duration a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes but is overridable per zone by the operations team."},{"term":"Pending Delivery","definition":"A delivery that has been created and assigned to a zone but not yet assigned to a driver's route or completed."},{"term":"Failed Delivery","definition":"A delivery attempt that was unsuccessful (e.g., customer not available), triggering route recalculation to remove or reschedule the stop."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"When a driver starts their shift, the system must automatically generate an optimized route from all pending deliveries in the driver's assigned zone.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational workflow trigger, not a data integrity constraint. The optimization algorithm and trigger timing are policy decisions.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN pending deliveries exist in zone X, WHEN a driver assigned to zone X starts their shift, THEN an optimized route containing all eligible pending deliveries is created and assigned to the driver within the system's SLA."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A driver's route must be recalculated when a new delivery is added to their zone while they are on an active shift.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Recalculation policy can be tuned (e.g., batching, cooldown periods) without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a driver has an active route in zone X, WHEN a new pending delivery is added to zone X, THEN the route is recalculated incorporating the new delivery while preserving already-completed stops."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"A driver's route must be recalculated when a delivery on their route is marked as failed.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Recalculation on failure is a policy; the system could instead just skip the stop.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a driver has an active route containing delivery D, WHEN delivery D is marked as failed, THEN the route is recalculated excluding delivery D from remaining stops."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A driver's route must be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Deviation handling is a business policy; alternative approaches (warning, ignoring) are valid choices.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a driver has an active route with next stop S, WHEN the driver skips S or visits a different stop, THEN the route is recalculated from the driver's current position with remaining undelivered stops."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Customers must be notified when their delivery enters the next-3-stops window of a driver's optimized route.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Notification window size (3 stops) is a configurable business preference, not a system integrity rule.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a driver is progressing through their route, WHEN a customer's delivery becomes one of the next 3 stops in the sequence, THEN the customer receives a notification."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"A driver must never be assigned a delivery that requires a vehicle class exceeding their licensed vehicle class.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Assigning a motorcycle driver a freight shipment is physically impossible and likely illegal. Violating this creates safety hazards, regulatory violations, and guaranteed delivery failures. This is a hard physical and legal constraint.","testable_assertion":"FOR every delivery D on a driver's route, D.requiredVehicleClass must be compatible with driver.licensedVehicleClass. No route — whether initial or recalculated — may ever contain an incompatible assignment."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"The default estimated stop time is 4 minutes per delivery stop, overridable by the operations team per zone.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Stop time is a tunable operational parameter, not a system constraint.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN no override exists for zone X, WHEN calculating route ETAs, THEN 4 minutes per stop is used. GIVEN an override of N minutes exists for zone X, WHEN calculating route ETAs, THEN N minutes per stop is used."},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"Route recalculation must not reorder or revisit stops that the driver has already completed.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Re-assigning completed deliveries corrupts delivery state and causes duplicate delivery attempts, wasting resources and confusing customers.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a driver has completed stops [A, B, C], WHEN route recalculation occurs, THEN the new route contains only uncompleted stops and completed stops are excluded."},{"id":"BR-9","rule":"A delivery can only appear on one driver's active route at a time.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Two drivers attempting the same delivery wastes resources, creates customer confusion, and corrupts delivery state tracking.","testable_assertion":"FOR any point in time, a delivery D that is assigned to driver X's active route must not appear on any other driver's active route."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A driver assigned to zone X starts their shift and there are 12 pending deliveries in zone X","when":"The shift is activated in the system","then":"An optimized route is generated containing all 12 deliveries, ordered to minimize travel, and assigned to the driver. Each delivery is marked as routed."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A driver has an active route with 8 remaining stops in zone X","when":"A 9th pending delivery is added to zone X","then":"The route is recalculated to include the new delivery among the remaining stops. Completed stops are untouched. The driver sees the updated route."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A driver has an active route and delivery D is the 5th of 8 remaining stops","when":"Delivery D is marked as failed","then":"The route is recalculated excluding D. Remaining stops are re-optimized. Customer for D is not notified of imminent delivery."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A driver's optimized route says next stop is Stop A","when":"The driver proceeds to Stop C instead (deviation)","then":"The system detects the deviation, recalculates the route from the driver's current position, and reorders remaining unvisited stops."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A driver is completing stops and delivery D is currently the 4th upcoming stop","when":"The driver completes a stop, making D now the 3rd upcoming stop","then":"Customer for delivery D receives a notification that their delivery is approaching."},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A motorcycle driver is assigned to zone X and a freight shipment is added to zone X","when":"Route calculation or recalculation runs","then":"The freight shipment is excluded from the motorcycle driver's route. The shipment remains pending for an eligible driver."},{"id":"AC-7","given":"Zone X has a stop-time override of 6 minutes set by operations","when":"A route is calculated for a driver in zone X","then":"All ETA calculations use 6 minutes per stop instead of the default 4 minutes."},{"id":"AC-8","given":"A driver has completed 5 of 10 stops and a recalculation is triggered","when":"The recalculation completes","then":"The new route contains exactly the 5 uncompleted stops. The 5 completed stops do not appear."},{"id":"AC-9","given":"A route recalculation changes the upcoming stop order such that customer C was in the next-3 window but is now stop #5","when":"The recalculated route is applied","then":"Customer C does not receive a duplicate notification. The system reflects the updated window accurately."},{"id":"AC-10","given":"A driver starts their shift in zone X but there are zero pending deliveries","when":"The shift is activated","then":"The driver is placed in an active-waiting state with no route. When a delivery is later added to zone X, a route is generated."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"No pending deliveries when driver starts shift","expected_behavior":"Driver enters an idle/waiting state. Route is generated as soon as the first delivery appears in the zone."},{"scenario":"Multiple recalculation triggers fire simultaneously (new delivery added + delivery failed at same time)","expected_behavior":"Recalculations must be serialized or debounced to avoid race conditions. The resulting route must reflect all triggers, not just the first."},{"scenario":"Driver deviates to a stop that IS on their route but out of order","expected_behavior":"System should recognize the stop as completed (not a true deviation to an unknown location), mark it done, and recalculate remaining stops from current position."},{"scenario":"Customer notification sent, then recalculation moves their delivery out of next-3 window","expected_behavior":"No retraction notification is explicitly specified. System must avoid re-notifying the same customer if they re-enter the window later. Idempotent notifications."},{"scenario":"All remaining deliveries on a route are marked as failed","expected_behavior":"Route becomes empty. Driver returns to idle/waiting state, eligible for new deliveries."},{"scenario":"A delivery is added to a zone with multiple active drivers","expected_behavior":"System must decide which driver gets the delivery. Assignment logic must consider proximity, current load, vehicle class, and route efficiency. Delivery must appear on exactly one route."},{"scenario":"Driver's shift ends while deliveries remain on route","expected_behavior":"Undelivered stops must be released back to pending status, available for reassignment. Customers in next-3 window who were notified may need a status update."},{"scenario":"Operations team changes zone stop-time override mid-shift","expected_behavior":"Should trigger route ETA recalculation for all active routes in the zone to maintain accurate arrival estimates."},{"scenario":"Recalculation cascade: recalculating route A causes a delivery to shift, triggering recalculation of route B","expected_behavior":"System must have cycle detection or a maximum recalculation depth to prevent infinite loops."},{"scenario":"Zone boundary delivery — delivery location is on the border of two zones","expected_behavior":"Delivery must be assigned to exactly one zone. Ambiguous zone membership must be resolved deterministically."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, increases deliveries per shift (higher throughput per driver), and reduces missed delivery costs. Vehicle class enforcement prevents costly failed delivery attempts and potential liability from safety violations.","operational":"Dynamic recalculation increases dispatcher efficiency by automating what was likely a manual process. However, frequent recalculations could cause driver confusion and route instability ('route thrashing'). The 4-minute default stop time creates a baseline for capacity planning.","user":"Customers benefit from proactive next-3-stops notifications, improving delivery experience and reducing 'where is my package' inquiries. Drivers benefit from automated route planning but may be frustrated by frequent route changes mid-shift.","risk":"Vehicle class mismatch is a safety and regulatory risk (HIGH). Route thrashing from excessive recalculations is an operational risk (MEDIUM). Notification accuracy impacts customer trust (MEDIUM). Zone assignment logic for multi-driver zones is an algorithmic fairness risk (LOW-MEDIUM).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Drivers","impact":"Core workflow change — shift from manual/dispatcher-planned routes to system-generated dynamic routes. Requires trust in optimization quality. Frequent recalculations could cause frustration."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Improved visibility via next-3-stops notifications. Higher delivery reliability from optimized routes. Risk of notification noise if recalculations are frequent."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"New configuration responsibility (zone stop-time overrides). Reduced manual dispatch workload. Need monitoring tools for route quality and recalculation frequency."},{"group":"Dispatchers","impact":"Role significantly changes — system handles primary route assignment. Dispatchers shift to exception handling and oversight."},{"group":"Fleet Managers","impact":"Vehicle class data must be accurate and up-to-date, as it now enforces hard constraints on delivery assignment."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The spec is well-structured but contains several ambiguities that materially affect implementation: the definition of 'deviation' is vague, optimization criteria are unspecified, multi-driver zone assignment logic is undefined, and the interaction between recalculation triggers and customer notifications needs clarification. No contradictions exist, but the ambiguities span critical operational paths.","high_confidence_areas":["Vehicle class constraint is a hard invariant — unambiguous and safety-critical","Recalculation trigger list is explicitly enumerated (3 triggers)","Stop time default and override mechanism is clearly specified","Customer notification trigger (next-3-stops) is well-defined","Completed stops must not be revisited — clear invariant"],"low_confidence_areas":["Optimization criteria (minimize distance? time? priority-weighted?) never stated","Multi-driver zone assignment — how to choose which driver gets a new delivery","Shift lifecycle — what happens to undelivered stops when shift ends","Notification idempotency — behavior when recalculation changes the next-3 window","Recalculation frequency limits — no debounce or throttle policy specified"],"assumptions":["A delivery belongs to exactly one zone and does not move between zones","A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift","Vehicle class compatibility is a strict hierarchy or categorization maintained in the Fleet context","Route optimization considers travel distance/time as the primary criterion","Notifications are one-way (push to customer) with no acknowledgment required"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Driver manually deviates from the suggested route' is undefined. Deviation could mean: (a) driver visits a stop out of sequence, (b) driver takes a different road between stops, (c) driver goes to a location not on their route. Each interpretation requires different detection logic (GPS geofencing vs. stop-sequence tracking) and produces different recalculation behavior.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Operations Team","Engineering"],"options":["Define deviation as visiting stops out of prescribed sequence (stop-order deviation)","Define deviation as GPS position diverging from prescribed path by threshold distance (geographic deviation)","Define deviation as both — out-of-sequence stop completion OR geographic divergence"],"recommendation":"Define deviation as stop-order deviation (visiting stops out of sequence or skipping a stop), as this is detectable without continuous GPS tracking and aligns with the route-as-stop-sequence model. Geographic path deviation between stops should not trigger recalculation."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Optimization criteria for route calculation are never specified. 'Optimized route' could mean shortest distance, shortest time, delivery-priority-weighted, or a hybrid. The choice fundamentally changes route behavior and customer experience.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Customers","Operations Team"],"options":["Optimize for shortest total travel time","Optimize for shortest total distance","Optimize for delivery priority (SLA-sensitive deliveries first) with time as tiebreaker","Multi-objective optimization with configurable weights per zone"],"recommendation":"Optimize for shortest total travel time with delivery-priority constraints (SLA deadlines must be met first, then minimize time). This balances efficiency with customer commitments."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"When multiple drivers are active in the same zone and a new delivery is added, the spec does not define which driver receives the delivery. This is a critical assignment decision that affects route quality, driver workload fairness, and delivery timeliness.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Customers","Operations Team","Dispatchers"],"options":["Assign to the driver with the fewest remaining stops (load balancing)","Assign to the driver geographically closest to the new delivery (proximity)","Assign to the driver whose route would increase least in total time (optimization-based)","Let dispatchers manually assign, with system suggestion"],"recommendation":"Use optimization-based assignment (least total time increase) with a load-balancing cap to prevent one driver from receiving disproportionate stops. This maximizes system efficiency while maintaining fairness."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The interaction between recalculation and the next-3-stops notification is ambiguous. If a customer was notified (entered the window), then a recalculation pushes them out of the window, should a retraction/update be sent? If they re-enter the window later, should they be notified again?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Operations Team"],"options":["Notifications are fire-and-forget — no retraction, no duplicate","Send update notification if customer leaves window after being notified","Notifications are idempotent — customer is notified at most once per delivery"],"recommendation":"Notifications should be idempotent (at-most-once per delivery). Once a customer is notified, they are not notified again for the same delivery regardless of window changes. No retraction notifications — they create more confusion than clarity."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
